learning_objectives: Define the core functions of an Electronic Health Record system
Differentiate between EHR, EMR, and PHR
Understand the ONC certification requirements for EHRs
readings: Electronic Health Records: Understanding and Using Computerized Medical Records Ch. 1-2
ONC Health IT Certification Program Overview

lecture_notes.introduction: Electronic Health Records are the backbone of modern healthcare delivery. This course provides hands-on experience with EHR systems while examining their design, implementation, and impact on clinical care.
key_concepts: EHR vs. EMR vs. PHR: scope and interoperability differences
IOM eight core EHR functions: health information, results management, order entry, decision support, communication, patient support, administrative processes, reporting
ONC certification criteria and testing programs
EHR market landscape: Epic, Cerner (Oracle Health), MEDITECH, athenahealth
Meaningful Use stages and their EHR requirements
lecture_notes.demonstration: Tour of a training EHR environment showing the patient chart, order entry, clinical documentation, and results review

review.value: EMR is a digital version of a single practice's chart; EHR is designed to share data across organizations and follow the patient
Federal program ensuring EHR products meet technical standards for interoperability, security, and clinical functionality

learning_objectives: Describe structured vs. unstructured clinical documentation
Understand documentation templates and smart phrases
Evaluate the impact of documentation requirements on clinician workflow
readings: AMA Reducing EHR Burden Position Paper

lecture_notes.introduction: Clinical documentation is both the most critical and most time-consuming function of the EHR. Getting it right impacts patient safety, billing, quality reporting, and clinician satisfaction.
key_concepts: Structured data entry: drop-downs, checkboxes, coded fields
Unstructured data: free-text notes, dictation, NLP extraction
SOAP note format in EHR documentation
Templates, macros, and smart phrases for efficiency
Documentation burden and the 'pajama time' problem
lecture_notes.demonstration: Hands-on exercise creating a clinical note using templates and smart phrases in the training EHR
setup_requirements: EHR training environment with documentation module

review.value: Structured data is machine-readable and queryable; unstructured requires NLP to extract meaning but captures clinical nuance
Subjective (patient report), Objective (exam findings), Assessment (diagnosis), Plan (treatment) - standard documentation framework

learning_objectives: Explain how CPOE improves order accuracy and safety
Describe types of clinical decision support interventions
Evaluate alert fatigue and CDS optimization strategies
readings: Bates DW. Clinical Decision Support Systems. BMJ 2020

lecture_notes.introduction: CPOE and Clinical Decision Support are where the EHR moves from a documentation tool to a patient safety system. When designed well, they prevent errors. When designed poorly, they contribute to alert fatigue.
key_concepts: CPOE workflow: order creation, clinical review, pharmacy verification
CDS types: alerts, reminders, order sets, info buttons, dashboards
Drug-drug interaction checking and allergy alerts
Alert fatigue: causes, measurement, and mitigation
CDS Five Rights: right information, right person, right format, right channel, right time
lecture_notes.demonstration: Entering medication orders in the training EHR and triggering CDS alerts for drug interactions and allergies
setup_requirements: EHR training environment with CPOE module
Sample patient with medication list and allergies

review.value: Phenomenon where clinicians become desensitized to excessive alerts, overriding both clinically important and unimportant warnings
Framework ensuring decision support delivers the right information to the right person in the right format through the right channel at the right time

learning_objectives: Describe the phases of an EHR implementation project
Understand change management principles for health IT adoption
Identify common implementation challenges and mitigation strategies
readings: KLAS Research: EHR Implementation Best Practices

lecture_notes.introduction: Implementing an EHR is one of the most complex projects a healthcare organization will undertake. Success depends as much on people and process as on technology.
key_concepts: Implementation phases: planning, design, build, testing, go-live, optimization
Governance structures: steering committee, workgroups, super users
Workflow analysis and redesign before system build
Training strategies: classroom, at-the-elbow, e-learning
Go-live support: command center, rounding, rapid response teams
lecture_notes.demonstration: Case study analysis of a failed EHR implementation and a successful one, identifying key differentiators

review.value: Clinical staff who receive advanced training and serve as departmental EHR experts and first-line support during and after go-live
Analyzing and optimizing clinical processes before configuring the EHR, rather than simply digitizing broken paper workflows

class_schedule.topic: Patient Portals and Consumer-Facing EHR Features
learning_objectives: Describe patient portal functionality and adoption drivers
Understand the 21st Century Cures Act and information blocking rules
Evaluate the impact of open notes and patient data access on care
readings: ONC Information Blocking Final Rule Summary

lecture_notes.introduction: Patients are increasingly active participants in their health data. The regulatory and technological landscape now demands transparent data sharing through patient-facing tools.
key_concepts: Patient portal features: view records, schedule visits, message providers, pay bills
OpenNotes movement and its evidence base
21st Century Cures Act: information blocking provisions
SMART on FHIR apps for patient data access
Health equity considerations in portal design and access
lecture_notes.demonstration: Patient portal walkthrough from the patient perspective, followed by the provider view of portal messages and requests

review.value: Practice that unreasonably prevents or interferes with access, exchange, or use of electronic health information - prohibited by Cures Act
Movement and now federal requirement for patients to have immediate access to their clinical notes through the patient portal

learning_objectives: Apply usability principles to evaluate EHR design
Understand the connection between EHR usability and patient safety
Develop strategies for ongoing EHR optimization

lecture_notes.introduction: A powerful EHR that clinicians cannot use effectively is a liability, not an asset. Usability engineering and continuous optimization are essential to realizing the EHR's potential.
key_concepts: Usability heuristics applied to EHR interfaces (Nielsen's 10 heuristics)
Cognitive load and its impact on clinical decision-making
EHR-related safety events and use-error classification
Optimization strategies: personalization, workflow streamlining, build governance
Measuring EHR efficiency: clicks, time, satisfaction surveys
lecture_notes.demonstration: Heuristic evaluation exercise on an EHR medication reconciliation screen, identifying usability issues and proposing improvements

review.value: The mental effort required to use the EHR - high cognitive load increases error risk and clinician burnout
Continuous process of refining EHR configuration, workflows, and training based on user feedback and performance data

questions.question: Explain the difference between structured and unstructured clinical documentation in an EHR. Why is the balance between the two important for both clinical care and data analytics?
questions.answer: Structured documentation uses coded, discrete data elements (drop-downs, checkboxes) that are easily queried and analyzed but may not capture clinical nuance. Unstructured documentation (free text, dictation) captures rich clinical detail but requires NLP to extract computable data. The balance matters because structured data enables quality reporting, CDS, and analytics, while unstructured text captures the clinical reasoning and context that structured fields miss. Over-reliance on either compromises data quality or documentation completeness.

rubric.key: Explains advantages and limitations of each

questions.question: What is alert fatigue in the context of Clinical Decision Support, and describe two evidence-based strategies for reducing it?
questions.answer: Alert fatigue occurs when clinicians are exposed to excessive CDS alerts, causing them to routinely dismiss warnings without reading them, including clinically important ones. Override rates exceeding 90% are common. Two strategies: (1) Tiered alerting - classify alerts by severity (hard stops for critical interactions, soft alerts for moderate, info-only for low) so clinicians learn which alerts deserve attention. (2) Context-aware filtering - suppress alerts that are not relevant to the clinical context (e.g., suppressing a drug interaction alert when the interacting drug was already discontinued).

rubric.key: Correct definition with override rate context

questions.question: Describe the role of 'super users' in an EHR implementation. Why are they considered more effective than external consultants for day-to-day support?
questions.answer: Super users are clinical staff members who receive advanced EHR training beyond the standard curriculum and serve as departmental experts during and after go-live. They are more effective than external consultants because they understand local clinical workflows and culture, are trusted peers to their colleagues, are available on the unit during regular shifts, can identify workflow issues from the clinical perspective, and remain in place after go-live for sustained optimization. They bridge the gap between IT and clinical operations.

rubric.key: Accurate definition of super user role
At least 3 reasons for effectiveness vs consultants
Understanding of bridge role between IT and clinical

questions.question: Explain the 21st Century Cures Act information blocking provisions. How do they change the relationship between patients and their health data?
questions.answer: The Cures Act information blocking provisions (effective April 2021) prohibit healthcare providers, health IT developers, and health information exchanges from practices that unreasonably prevent or interfere with access, exchange, or use of electronic health information. This fundamentally changes the patient-data relationship by giving patients the right to access all their electronic health information without delay, including clinical notes (OpenNotes), through standardized APIs (FHIR). Providers can no longer withhold records to keep patients in-network, and patients can authorize third-party apps to access their data.

rubric.key: Accurate description of information blocking rules
Identifies who is covered (providers, developers, HIEs)

major_assignments.title: EHR Usability Evaluation and Redesign Proposal
major_assignments.description: Conduct a heuristic usability evaluation of an EHR module (e.g., medication reconciliation, discharge summary, or order entry) using the training environment. Document usability issues, assess their severity, and propose redesign improvements.
objectives: Apply usability heuristics to evaluate healthcare software
Identify usability issues that impact clinical safety and efficiency
Understand the connection between EHR design and clinical outcomes
deliverables: Usability evaluation report (8-10 pages) with annotated screenshots
Redesign mockups or wireframes for top 3 issues
Presentation to class (15 minutes) with Q&A

criteria: Evidence linking issues to safety risks
Thorough usability evaluation using established heuristics
Clinically meaningful redesign proposals with mockups
Evidence-based analysis connecting design to safety

weekly_quizzes.description: Assessment of EHR concepts, functionality, and implementation knowledge

hands_on_labs.description: Practical exercises in the EHR training environment

midterm_examination.description: Comprehensive assessment of EHR core functions and clinical workflows

final_examination.description: Cumulative exam including implementation, usability, and patient access topics

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is mandatory for all hands-on lab sessions. More than 2 unexcused lab absences will result in a grade reduction.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late assignments lose 10% per day up to 5 days. Lab exercises must be completed during the scheduled session.
course_policies.collaboration: Lab exercises may be completed in pairs. All written assignments and evaluations must be individual work.
course_policies.academic_integrity: Screenshots and work from the training EHR must not include real patient data. All analysis must be original with proper citations.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Laptop with internet access. EHR training environment credentials will be provided. Screen capture software for usability evaluation.
